Title: Why does Scheme allow mutation to closed environment in a closure? - Stack Overflow
URL: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/13371530/why-does-scheme-allow-mutation-to-closed-environment-in-a-closure
Content: ### more stack exchange communities. Stack Overflow for Teams is now called **Stack Internal**. Allowing subsequent mutation to affect bindings in the closed environment in a closure seems to revert to kinda dynamic scoping. Lexical scope guarantees you that the reference to `x` always points to the binding of `x` in the `let` binding. Conceptually, the `let` binding is actually creating a new location in memory (containing `1`) and *that location* is the value bound to `x`. In contrast, dynamic scope allows any code to change the value you're referring to in `f`, regardless of whether you gave access to the location bound to `x`. The `x` variable refers to a memory cell in the internal environment frame belonging to that `let` *in which* the internal `lambda` is defined - thus returning the combination of `lambda` and its defining environment, otherwise known as "closure". The variable `x` that `f` refers to, lives in the top-level environment, and hence is accessible from any code in the same scope.

Title: cs61a-projects/scheme/scheme.py at master - GitHub
URL: https://github.com/MattZhao/cs61a-projects/blob/master/scheme/scheme.py
Content: """A Scheme interpreter and its read-eval-print loop.""". def scheme\_eval(expr, env, \_=None): # Optional third argument is ignored. >>> expr = read\_line("(+ 2 2)"). >>> scheme\_eval(expr, create\_global\_frame()). return scheme\_atomp(expr) or scheme\_stringp(expr) or expr is okay. def scheme\_apply(procedure, args, env):. """Apply Scheme PROCEDURE to argument values ARGS in environment ENV.""". return apply\_primitive(procedure, args, env). new\_env = make\_call\_frame(procedure, args, env). def eval\_all(expressions, env):. """Evaluate a Scheme list of EXPRESSIONS & return the value of the last.""". scheme\_eval(expressions[x], env). return scheme\_eval(expressions[len(expressions)-1], env, True). def apply\_primitive(procedure, args\_scheme\_list, env):. """Apply PrimitiveProcedure PROCEDURE to ARGS\_SCHEME\_LIST in ENV. args = list(args\_scheme\_list) # Convert a Scheme list to a Python list. def make\_call\_frame(procedure, args, env):. """Make a frame that binds the formal paramters of PROCEDURE to ARGS.""". new\_f = procedure.env.make\_child\_frame(procedure.formals, args). new\_f = env.make\_child\_frame(procedure.formals, args). in a Scheme list of formal parameters FORMALS are bound to the Scheme. a Scheme list of BODY expressions, and a parent environment that. env.bindings[target] = scheme\_eval(expressions[1], env). if scheme\_true(scheme\_eval(expressions[0], env)):. return scheme\_eval(expressions[1],env, True).

Title: CS61A Proj 4 - Scheme Interpreter - Misaka's blog
URL: https://www.misaka-9982.com/2022/12/17/CS61A-Proj-4
Content: >>> expr = read_line('(+ 2 2)') >>> expr Pair('+', Pair(2, Pair(2, nil))) >>> scheme_eval(expr, create_global_frame())  4  """  # Evaluate atoms if scheme_symbolp(expr): return env.lookup(expr) elif self_evaluating(expr):  return expr  # All non-atomic expressions are lists (combinations) if not scheme_listp(expr): raise SchemeError('malformed list: {0}'.format(repl_str(expr))) first, rest = expr.first, expr.rest if scheme_symbolp(first) and first in scheme_forms.SPECIAL_FORMS: return scheme_forms.SPECIAL_FORMSfirst else:  # BEGIN PROBLEM 3 "*** YOUR CODE HERE ***" procedure = scheme_eval(first, env) args = rest.map(lambda operand: scheme_eval(operand, env)) return scheme_apply(procedure, args, env)  # END PROBLEM 3 def pair_to_list(pair): if pair is nil: return [] else: return [pair.first] + pair_to_list(pair.rest) ``` |. (define x (+ 1 2)) validate_form(expressions, 2, 2) # Checks that expressions is a list of length exactly 2  # BEGIN PROBLEM 4 "*** YOUR CODE HERE ***" value = scheme_eval(expressions.rest.first, env) env.define(signature, value)  return signature  # END PROBLEM 4 elif isinstance(signature, Pair) and scheme_symbolp(signature.first): # defining a named procedure e.g.

Title: CS 61A Scheme Specification | CS 61A Spring 2022
URL: https://cs61a.vercel.app/articles/scheme-spec/index.html
Content: The version of Scheme used in this course is not perfectly true to any official specification of the language, though it is perhaps closest to R5RS, with some features and terminology from other versions, both older and newer. This document and the linked built-in procedure reference are very long and are an attempt to formalize the variant of Scheme used in 61A. You should not find it necessary to read the full references of special forms and built-in procedures, but specific sections may be helpful to reference when working on Scheme assignments and the project (the relevant sections will typically be linked in the assignment itself). Some expressions are **self-evaluating**, which means they are both an expression and a value, and that it evaluates to itself. When looking up a symbol in an environment, Scheme first checks the current frame and returns the corresponding value if it exists.
